pests.name: Fall Armyworm
pests.description: Fall Armyworm is the most destructive pest of maize in Lesotho, thriving during the warm, wet summer season (November to March). It is a moth whose larvae cause severe damage by feeding on leaves and burrowing into the whorl and cobs of maize plants.
symptoms: Ragged holes in leaves with a characteristic 'window-pane' pattern
Pinholes in young whorl leaves
Presence of frass (sawdust-like droppings) in the whorl
Damaged or missing tassels
Larvae visible inside the whorl, especially early morning or evening
Skeletonized leaves in severe infestations
symptoms_st: Masoba a makhasing a nang le sebopeho sa 'window-pane'
Masoba a manyenyane makhasing a mocha
Tšila ea larva ka hare ho whorl
Tassels tse senyehileng kapa tse siileng
Larva e bonoa ka hare ho whorl matsatsi a hoseng kapa mantsiboea

chemical: Apply emamectin benzoate (e.g., Coragen, Proclaim) into the whorl at 5-10% infestation
Chlorpyrifos or lambda-cyhalothrin as foliar sprays
Spinosad-based insecticides are effective and less harmful to natural enemies
biological: Apply Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) formulations into the whorl at early infestation stages
Encourage natural enemies: Telenomus remus (egg parasitoid), Chelonus sp., and predatory beetles
Apply entomopathogenic fungi such as Beauveria bassiana
cultural: Early planting before November to avoid peak moth flight
Plant push-pull companion crops: Napier grass as border crop, Desmodium as intercrop
Hand-pick egg masses and young larvae early in the season
Apply sand or ash mixed with soil into the whorl to deter larvae
Apply neem extract (10 ml neem oil per liter of water with a few drops of soap) into the whorl
treatment_st: Jala kapele place pele ho Phato ho qoba ho falla ha moth ka boraro bo setseng
Sebelisa tholoana ea neem (jele neem ka 10 ml ho litra ea metsi le lithe tse 'maloa tsa sesepa) ka hare ho whorl
Tšela moqoqo kapa lesoha le kopantsoeng le mobu ka hare ho whorl
Nka mahe le larva ka letsoho bofelong ba selemo

prevention: Rotate crops each season - avoid consecutive maize planting
Plant early maturing maize varieties (before November)
Use resistant/tolerant maize varieties where available
Scout fields at least twice per week during vegetative stage
Use pheromone traps to monitor adult moth populations
Maintain field sanitation - destroy crop residues after harvest
Intercrop maize with beans or cowpeas to disrupt pest habitat
pests.economic_threshold: Apply control measures when 5-10% of plants show infestation in the whorl stage, or when 2+ larvae per plant are found.
pests.monitoring: Inspect 20 plants per field site (at least 5 sites across the field). Check whorl for frass, larvae, and leaf damage. Scout in early morning when larvae are more visible.
pests.lesotho_context: Fall Armyworm has been present in Lesotho since around 2017 and is now a major seasonal threat, particularly in the lowlands and foothills. The Lesotho Meteorological Services monitors its spread through seasonal agrometeorological bulletins. Peak risk is during warm, humid periods from December to February.

pests.name: Maize Stalk Borer
pests.description: The African maize stalk borer is one of the most economically important pests of maize and sorghum in Lesotho and sub-Saharan Africa. Larvae tunnel into plant stems and can cause total crop loss if uncontrolled.
symptoms: Dead heart symptom in young plants (central whorl dries and turns brown)
Tiny pinholes in leaf sheaths caused by early instar larvae
Frass at the base of the plant or inside stalks
Broken tassels due to stalk tunneling
Stem breakage at heading stage
Visible entry holes at stem nodes
Late-season symptom: cob damage and ear rot
symptoms_st: Mokopu o omileng o mosuoe ho litšalo tse ncha (dead heart)
Masoba a manyenyane makhasing a mocha
Tšila ka hare ho stalk kapa fatshe ho tsala
Tassels tse senyehileng ka lebaka la tunneling ea stalk

chemical: Apply granular insecticides (e.g., carbofuran, chlorpyrifos granules) into the whorl at early stage
Foliar spray with lambda-cyhalothrin or cypermethrin when egg hatching begins
Repeat applications every 10-14 days during vegetative stage if infestation persists
biological: Apply Cotesia sesamiae (parasitic wasp) for biological control
Use Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis var. kurstaki) granules applied to the whorl
Encourage natural predators such as birds, ground beetles, and spiders
cultural: Early planting to escape peak moth oviposition period
Harvest and destroy crop residues promptly after harvest
Deep plowing to expose and kill pupae in soil
Intercropping with legumes (beans, cowpeas) to create less favorable habitat
Ash application into the whorl as a low-cost deterrent
treatment_st: Jala kapele ho qoba nako ea boholo ba bofolisi ba moth
Chemsa le ho chetsa masala a lijalo ka pela ka mora kotulo
Lema ka botebo ho senya li-pupae tse mobung
Tšela lesoha ka hare ho whorl joalo ka thibelo e itekanetseng moputso

prevention: Deep tillage to destroy pupae in crop residues
Crop rotation (avoid maize after maize in same field)
Timely planting - align with optimal planting window
Use of Bt maize varieties where registered
Regular field scouting from emergence to tasseling
Remove and burn crop residues after harvest
Push-pull cropping system with Napier grass and Desmodium
pests.economic_threshold: Action required when dead heart symptoms exceed 5% of plants, or when 1+ egg mass per 10 plants is found during scouting.
pests.monitoring: Scout from crop emergence to 6-leaf stage. Look for dead hearts, egg masses on leaf undersides, and frass at stem base. Check 20-30 plants per hectare at multiple sites.
pests.lesotho_context: Maize stalk borer is endemic to Lesotho and has been a persistent threat throughout the growing season. Field inspections by LMS in January 2026 documented high stalk borer incidents in Thaba-Tseka district, with farmers forced to replant in some areas.

pests.name: Cereal Aphids
pests.season: Dry season (May-September), can occur November-February on maize
pests.description: Several aphid species attack cereals in Lesotho. The corn leaf aphid attacks maize, while the sorghum aphid attacks sorghum. They suck sap from leaves and can transmit plant viruses. Large infestations cause direct damage and indirectly affect yield through honeydew and sooty mould.
symptoms: Yellowing and curling of leaves
Sticky honeydew deposits on leaves
Black sooty mould growing on honeydew
Visible colonies of small soft-bodied insects on undersides of leaves
Ant activity on plants (ants tend aphids for honeydew)
Stunted plant growth in severe infestations
Premature leaf senescence
symptoms_st: Makhasi a mosehla le a phurulohanang
Monko o khotle wa honeydew makhasing
Hloibila e ntso e holang honeydewing
Likhele tse nyane tse bonehang ka tlas'a makhasi

chemical: Imidacloprid or thiamethoxam seed treatment for early protection
Foliar spray with dimethoate or pirimicarb when colonies exceed threshold
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill natural enemies (ladybirds, lacewings)
biological: Encourage ladybird beetles (Coccinellidae) - major natural predators
Lacewings and parasitic wasps (Aphidius spp.) provide natural control
Neem extract spray (10ml/liter) disrupts aphid reproduction
Strong water jet spray to dislodge aphid colonies from plants
cultural: Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization which promotes lush growth attractive to aphids
Remove weeds that serve as alternate aphid hosts
Plant trap crops such as barley or susceptible varieties around field borders
treatment_st: Qoba manyolo a mangata a nitrogen a hotsang ho hola ha likhele
Tlosa moqoqo o sebelang joalo ka moabeli o mong oa likhele
Fafatsa motsoako oa neem (10ml/liter) ho thibela ho tsoala ha likhele

prevention: Plant resistant or tolerant varieties
Avoid excessive use of nitrogen fertilizers
Maintain balanced plant nutrition
Encourage biodiversity around fields to support natural enemies
Regular monitoring to catch infestations early
Avoid late planting which increases aphid pressure
pests.economic_threshold: Control needed when aphid colonies cover >50% of upper leaves before tasseling, or >50% of leaves at grain fill.
pests.monitoring: Check underside of leaves and growing points weekly. Count colonies and look for natural enemy presence (ladybirds, lacewing larvae). Also watch for ants tending aphids.
pests.lesotho_context: Aphid infestations in Lesotho are typically worse during dry spells within the growing season. The 2018-2019 season saw aphid pressure in the south, where dry spell conditions prevailed.

pests.name: Bean Bruchid / Bean Weevil
pests.description: Bean bruchids are storage pests that damage dry beans and cowpeas. Infestation often starts in the field before harvest, but damage becomes catastrophic in storage if not controlled. A single generation can destroy an entire stored seed stock.
symptoms: Round exit holes in bean seeds (3-4mm diameter)
Presence of fine powdery dust (frass) in storage
Small white eggs laid on or inside seed coats
Visible adult beetles in storage containers
Hollow, lightweight seeds when shaken
Characteristic musty smell in infested storage
symptoms_st: Masoba a moniloano menyolelong ea linaoa (3-4mm)
Phofu e monyenyane e boputsoane (tšila) polokellong
Mahe a masoeu a kopileng hodima kapa ka hare ho thapo ea pere
Linonyana tse holletsoeng ka hare ho ncha tse bolokisoang
Monko o sa nkeheng kapa ho bola polokellong e kenang

chemical: Phosphine fumigation in sealed storage (professional use only)
Dust storage with approved grain protectant powders (malathion dust - check local registration)
Insecticide treatment of storage bags before filling
biological: Mix dried neem leaves with stored beans (5kg neem leaves per 100kg beans)
Apply diatomaceous earth (DE) at 1g per kg of grain - physically damages beetle exoskeleton
Wood ash mixed with grain - traditional and effective method
Bay leaves mixed with stored beans deter adult beetles
cultural: Use hermetic (airtight) storage bags (PICS bags, metal silos) to suffocate beetles
Sun-dry beans thoroughly (moisture below 12%) before storage
Harvest beans before pods dry completely to reduce field infestation
Clean storage containers thoroughly before new storage
Store in cool, dark location to slow beetle development
treatment_st: Sebelisa mekotla e koalehang (PICS bags, lipoto tsa tšepe) ho bolaya linonyana ka ho tlala moya
Omisa linaoa hantle (moisture e tlase ho 12%) pele ho boloka
Kopanya makhasi a neem a omileng le linaoa tse bolokiloeng (5kg neem ho 100kg linaoa)
Sebelisa lesoha la pere kapa diatomaceous earth ho sireletsa linaoa

prevention: Harvest at optimal maturity to minimize field infestation
Dry beans thoroughly before storage
Use hermetic storage systems
Inspect stored grain monthly
Never mix new beans with previously stored (possibly infested) beans
Keep storage facilities clean and free of old grain debris
pests.economic_threshold: Control needed when more than 2-3 adult beetles are found per kg of stored grain, or at first sign of exit holes in seeds.
pests.monitoring: Inspect storage monthly. Sift a sample of grain through a fine mesh to catch adults. Look for exit holes, frass, and live adults. Monitor during first 3 months after storage.
pests.lesotho_context: Bean bruchid is a serious post-harvest problem for subsistence farmers in Lesotho who store beans from one season to the next for food and seed. Traditional ash storage methods are commonly used and are effective.

pests.name: Locusts
pests.description: Locusts can devastate crops in swarms covering many hectares within hours. Both migratory and desert locusts have been recorded in southern Africa. In Lesotho, they pose a seasonal risk especially in drought years. LMS field reports from 2026 documented locust damage to wheat in Malefiloane, Mokhotlong.
symptoms: Complete defoliation of crops and pasture
Large numbers of insects visible in swarms (many millions per km²)
Stripped plant stems with leaves fully consumed
Damage often follows a directional pattern across fields
Crop loss can be 100% within a swarm path
symptoms_st: Ho tlosoa ha makhasi ohle a lijalo le mahlaba
Liphatsa tsa likhala tsa hloile li bonehang ka lipota (milione tse ngata ka km²)
Metsu ea limela e hatakiloeng makhasi ohle a jeoeng
Tahlehelo ea lijalo e ka ba 100% ka mokhahlelong oa pota

chemical: Aerial or ground spraying with organophosphate insecticides (malathion, chlorpyrifos) - requires coordination with national authorities
Ultra-low-volume (ULV) application of deltamethrin or lambda-cyhalothrin
Emergency pesticide application coordinated with Ministry of Agriculture and FAO
biological: Apply Metarhizium acridum (Green Muscle) - a fungus specific to locusts, safe for other organisms
Effective for hopper bands (nymphs) rather than adult swarms
cultural: Early warning: monitor FAO Desert Locust Information Service (DLIS) and LMS bulletins
Coordinate with neighbors and extension officers for community response
Drive swarms away using noise and smoke before they land
Harvest any mature crops immediately if swarm approaching
treatment_st: Tseba kapele: sheba litaba tsa FAO le LMS ka mahloko
Lumellana le bapi le likantoro tsa temo bakeng sa karabo ea sechaba
Qosetsa lipota ka modumo le musi pele li tlohela
Kotula lijalo tse holo ha holo haeba pota e atamela

prevention: Monitor LMS seasonal forecasts and FAO locust bulletins
Report swarm sightings to extension officers immediately
Participate in community early warning networks
Keep contact with Ministry of Agriculture for coordinated response plans
pests.economic_threshold: Any locust swarm sighting in a field is an emergency requiring immediate action and reporting to authorities.
pests.monitoring: Watch for locust swarms on the horizon. Monitor FAO locust tracking alerts. Report any sightings to the Ministry of Agriculture and LMS immediately.
pests.lesotho_context: LMS field inspection reports from early 2026 documented locust damage to wheat crops in Malefiloane area of Mokhotlong district, where farmers reported that locusts fed on their wheat. This is consistent with regional locust activity in southern Africa.

pests.name: Wheat & Maize Rust
pests.season: Cool, moist periods (April-August for wheat rust)
pests.description: Rust diseases caused by Puccinia fungi are major threats to wheat in Lesotho highlands. Stem rust (Puccinia graminis), yellow rust (Puccinia striiformis) and leaf rust (Puccinia triticina) all occur. Outbreaks are favored by cool, wet conditions and can spread rapidly across fields.
symptoms: Orange-red or brown pustules on leaves (leaf rust)
Yellow stripes of pustules on leaves (yellow rust/stripe rust)
Dark brown/black elongated pustules on stems (stem rust)
Pustules burst and release powdery spores
Premature leaf death in severe cases
Shriveled, lightweight grain at harvest
symptoms_st: Li-pustule tse pinki-mofubelu kapa tse bothsa makhasing (lehlabathe la makhasi)
Mela e mosehla ea li-pustule makhasing (lehlabathe la mosehla)
Li-pustule tse ntso tse telele lithemong (lehlabathe la themo)
Li-pustule li phatloha le ho ntša spore e boputsoane

chemical: Foliar fungicide application - triazoles (propiconazole, tebuconazole) are most effective
Strobilurin fungicides (azoxystrobin, trifloxystrobin) provide good protection
Apply at first sign of rust pustules and repeat in 14-21 days
Protectant fungicide (mancozeb) if resistant varieties unavailable
biological: No effective biological controls currently available for rusts
Resistant varieties are the primary biological approach
cultural: Plant resistant wheat varieties (consult extension officer for current registered varieties)
Avoid late planting which exposes crops to rust-favorable conditions
Ensure adequate plant nutrition - balanced NPK reduces disease susceptibility
Remove volunteers and grass weeds that harbor rust between seasons
treatment_st: Sebelisa mefuta e hanyetsanang ea koro (botsa mooki oa temo bakeng sa mefuta e ngolisitsoeng)
Qoba ho jala ka morao ho koana ho hlahisang lijalo tšebetsong ea maemo a rusta
Fumana phepo e lekaneng ea limela - NPK e lekanetsoeng e theola bokuli

prevention: Use rust-resistant wheat varieties as primary defense
Plant at the recommended time for your area
Avoid volunteer plants from previous season
Ensure good air circulation (avoid very high plant densities)
Monitor weekly for early rust pustules
Apply fungicide protectant before rust appears in high-risk years
pests.economic_threshold: Apply fungicide when rust is first detected. For stripe rust: treat when 5% of leaves show symptoms. For stem rust: treat immediately at first sign.
pests.monitoring: Examine flag leaf and upper 2-3 leaves from crop emergence to grain fill. Look for telltale yellow stripes (stripe rust) or orange powder when leaves are rubbed (leaf rust). Check at multiple field sites.
pests.lesotho_context: Rust diseases are a significant concern for wheat farmers in the Lesotho highlands and mountains. The cool, moist highland climate is particularly conducive to yellow rust development. Extension officers can advise on current resistant variety availability.

pests.name: Hailstorm Damage
pests.description: Hailstorms are a recurring and devastating weather event in Lesotho during the summer growing season. LMS records documented devastating hailstorms in November 2025 (Maletsunyane area) and January 2026 (Mokhotlong, Khubelu valley) that caused significant livestock and crop losses.
symptoms: Shredded, torn leaves - resembles insect damage but more widespread
Bruising and cuts on stems and stalks
Damaged or broken cobs, ears, and pods
Flattened crops in the direction of storm track
Sudden widespread damage across entire fields after storms
Entry points for fungal diseases at wounds
symptoms_st: Makhasi a seputsoeng, a phathalletsoe - e ts'oana le tšenyo ea likhala empa e atoloha haholo
Letšoao le lihaqosong le lithemong
Likhoaba, matoeba le likotla tse senyehileng
Lijalo tse oeletseng ka ketso ea seoelo
Tšenyo e atolohileng ka sebosetso kamora lipoloko

chemical: Apply fungicide after hail to prevent fungal infection at wound sites
Foliar nutrients (zinc, boron) help plants recover faster
biological: Allow plants to recover naturally - maize has strong recovery if damage is before tasseling
Do not replant immediately - assess recovery over 7-10 days first
cultural: Assess damage extent before deciding to replant
Remove badly damaged plants to reduce disease risk
If replanting is needed, consider shorter-season varieties
Contact extension officer and local government to report and document losses
Consider crop insurance schemes if available
treatment_st: Hlola boholo ba tšenyo pele o etsa qeto ea ho lema bocha
Tlosa limela tse senyehileng haholo ho fokotsa kotsi ea bokuli
Haeba ho lema bocha ho hlokahala, nahana ka mefuta ea motse o moholo ka potlako e nyenyane
Ikopanye le mooki oa temo le mmuso wa lehae ho tlaleha le ho ngola tahlehelo

prevention: Monitor LMS weather forecasts and warnings daily during summer season
Avoid planting in known hail-prone valley areas if possible
Early planting to reduce exposure during peak hail season
Use protective netting for high-value vegetable crops
Keep LMS/LNBS contact numbers for weather warnings
pests.economic_threshold: Any hailstorm event requires immediate field assessment. Report severe damage to local extension offices for government support.
pests.monitoring: Check fields within 24 hours after any hailstorm. Photograph and document damage for insurance or disaster relief claims. Monitor for fungal diseases in the 2 weeks following hail.
pests.lesotho_context: Hailstorms are a recurring agricultural hazard in Lesotho. The LMS December 2025 bulletin documented devastating hailstorms on 23rd November 2025 in Maletsunyane constituency (massive sheep losses). In January 2026, hail destroyed maize fields in the Khubelu valley in Mokhotlong, with multiple villages affected. Farmers are advised to monitor LMS daily weather bulletins and LNBS for hail warnings.

pests.name: Cutworms
pests.description: Cutworms are soil-dwelling moth larvae that cut seedlings at ground level, causing sudden stand losses early in the season. They are most damaging during early crop establishment and are active at night.
symptoms: Seedlings cut off at soil level overnight
Wilted seedlings with no visible above-ground pest
Soil disturbance near plant base
C-shaped grey or brown larvae found in soil around affected plants
Random gaps in plant stand in the morning
symptoms_st: Litšalo tse sekoalloa fatshe bosiu
Litšalo tse nyametsoeng ntle le pest e bonahala ka hodima mobu
Larva e shapang nkeng e bonoa ha o phula mobu haufi le limela tse amehileng

chemical: Soil incorporation of chlorpyrifos or carbosulfan before planting
Bait application: mix insecticide with bran and molasses for night application
Chlorpyrifos drench around plant base for established infestations
biological: Entomopathogenic nematodes (Steinernema spp.) applied to moist soil
Diatomaceous earth applied around plant bases
cultural: Deep plowing exposes pupae to predators and sun before planting
Flood irrigation or heavy watering to force larvae to surface for bird predation
Hand-pick larvae from soil around affected plants at night or at sunrise
Delay planting by 2-3 weeks after first rains to allow larvae to feed on weeds first
treatment_st: Lema ka botebo ho hlahisa li-pupae ho linonyana le letsatsi pele ho jala
Phuthisa metsi a mangata ho qobella larva ho fihlela holimo bakeng sa linonyana
Nka larva ka letsoho mobu haufi le limela tse amehileng bosiu kapa hoseng

prevention: Deep plowing before planting to expose and destroy pupae
Early planting before cutworm populations build up
Clean field borders and remove weeds before planting
Use seed dressings with insecticide where cutworms are a known problem
pests.economic_threshold: Replant or treat when cutworm damage causes >5% stand loss in young seedlings.
pests.monitoring: Inspect newly emerged seedlings daily in the first 2-3 weeks after planting. If seedlings are cut, dig in soil around the base to find larvae. Count affected plants per row.
pests.lesotho_context: Cutworms are a common problem in Lesotho at planting time (October-November). They are favored by cool, moist soil conditions and are worse in fields with high organic matter or previous grass cover.

crop_rotation.en: Rotating crops breaks pest and disease cycles. Avoid planting the same crop in the same field for consecutive seasons. A common rotation in Lesotho: maize → beans → sorghum → fallow.
crop_rotation.st: Ho potoloha lijalo ho phá lipotoloho tsa likokonyana le makulane. Qoba ho lema sejalo se le seng sebakeng se le seng lilemong tse latellanang.

field_sanitation.en: Remove and destroy crop residues after harvest to eliminate pest overwintering sites. Burn or deeply bury infested material. Keep field edges and borders free of weeds.
field_sanitation.st: Tlosa le ho chetsa masala a lijalo ka mora kotulo ho felisa libaka tsa likokonyana. Chesa kapa patela haholo thepa e kenang. Boloka meeli ea masimo e hlokile moqoqo.

early_detection.en: Scout fields at least weekly. Check 20 plants per site at 5+ locations across the field. Keep a scouting record. Early detection allows early intervention before populations explode.
early_detection.st: Hlahloba masimo beke le beke. Sheba limela tse 20 sebakeng se seng le se seng ho 5+ libaka tsa tšimo. Boloka rekoto ea hlahloba. Ho lemoa kapele ho lumella ho kenya letsoho pele motho a hola.

integrated_pest_management.en: IPM combines cultural, biological, and chemical methods. Start with cultural and biological methods; use chemicals only as a last resort. This protects the environment, reduces costs, and preserves natural pest enemies.
integrated_pest_management.st: IPM e kopanya mekhoa ea setso, ea bioloji le ea lik'hemik'hale. Qala ka mekhoa ea setso le ea bioloji; sebelisa lik'hemik'hale feela joalo ka mohato oa ho qetela.

weather_monitoring.en: Monitor LMS (Lesotho Meteorological Services) agrometeorological bulletins regularly. Pest outbreaks often correlate with specific weather patterns - warm, wet periods favor Fall Armyworm and fungal diseases; dry spells favor aphids.
weather_monitoring.st: Sheba litataiso tsa LMS (Lesotho Meteorological Services) ka mehla. Merusu ea likokonyana hangata e tšoanela le maemo a leaera - nako e futhumetseng le e nang le pula e khetha Fall Armyworm le makulane a bioloji; nako e omileng e khetha likhele.
